column2: The Loring Park Development is a tax-increment-financed urban renewal project covering nine blocks (about 40 acres) in downtown Minneapolis adjacent to Loring Park and the central business district. It is planned to deliver roughly 2,000 housing units, 800-1,100 hotel units, and supporting commercial space, all linked by a raised pedestrian Greenway connecting an extension of the Nicollet Mall to Loring Park. Key components include the 102-unit Loring 100 Section 8 elderly housing project (the HUD action triggering this EIS), Nicollet Towers, townhouses, and a Hyatt Regency convention hotel, …
column7: The extraction contains several specific figures not supported by the visible cited pages: hotel units are stated as '800-1,100' while the source clearly states '1050'; '$21 million in general obliga…

column2: Before redevelopment, the project area held about 1,000 residents in older walk-up apartments and rooming houses, with a largely low-income, single-person, and elderly population (51% earning under $4,000) and a high crime rate more than five times the city average. About 921 persons and 22 small businesses were slated for relocation, and most displaced residents could not afford the new market-rate units; many were elderly tenants of buildings such as the Naulhaka Flats, Imperial, Elmhurst, and Roosevelt Hotel. Surrounding institutions include Loring Park itself, Eitel and Abbott hospitals, …

The EIS evaluates a wide range of alternatives, including no project, building only on already-cleared parcels, rehabilitating existing structures (especially the Naulhaka Flats), and changing land use to office or industrial. Density and design alternatives include reduced density with more family townhouses, increased high-rise density, and a low-rise/high-density (roughly 50 units per acre) option, along with dispersing subsidized units instead of concentrating them and adding more family rather than elderly subsidized housing. Other alternatives address project staging, alternative financ…

Adverse impacts include displacement of low-income and elderly residents, demolition of older but potentially rehabilitable buildings, shadow and wind effects from tall hotel and apartment towers, and short-term construction dust and noise. Vacating Willow, Harmon, 13th, and Spruce streets is expected to shift traffic onto LaSalle, Marquette, Hennepin, and 12th Street, risking carbon monoxide violations at intersections such as 12th/LaSalle and producing noise levels above state standards on most parcels. Beneficial impacts include separation of pedestrians from vehicles, energy-efficient hou…

Federal and state agencies including EPA, DOT, the Department of the Interior, the Minnesota Pollution Control Agency, and the Metropolitan Council raised concerns about air quality from street closures, noise standards, energy conservation, bicycle facilities, and overconcentration of subsidized elderly housing, with EPA giving the project an 'environmental reservations' rating. Citizen and neighborhood groups such as Citizens for a Loring Park Community, the Loring-Nicollet Community Council, the Naulhaka Flats Residents Association, the Elliot Park Neighborhood, and the Harmon-Hennepin Com…
